claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the child's age, and we'll continue from there.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and I'd like to know if I'm eligible. 

I actually have two children I'm asking about. Alex is 13 and Blake is 15, so they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Since both Alex (13) and Blake (15) are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex and Blake's work status:**

Are both Alex and Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This question is mainly relevant for older children, but I need to confirm this for both of them to continue.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, they're both just kids still - neither of them work at all. Alex is only 13, so definitely not working, and Blake doesn't have a job either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they meet this requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about benefits:**

Are both Alex and Blake **NOT receiving** any of the following benefits in their own name?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(This would be unusual for children their age, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits in their own names. They're just regular kids - no ESA or Universal Credit or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Let's continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about apprenticeships:**

Are Alex and Blake currently in any apprenticeships?

Most apprenticeships would disqualify them from Child Benefit, with one exception being Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales. But given their ages (13 and 15), I'd expect they're not in apprenticeships.

Are both children **NOT in an apprenticeship**, or are they perhaps in school/education as normal?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, they're both just in regular school. No apprenticeships - they're too young for that sort of thing anyway!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! Since both Alex and Blake are in regular school and not in any apprenticeships, they meet this requirement too. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check your residency status.**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you need to meet one of the following residency requirements. Do **any** of these apply to you?

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively looking for work
5. **You have pre-settled status** and have sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad

The most common situation is simply living in the UK. Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester with the kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to confirm you're responsible for Alex and Blake.**

You qualify as "responsible" for a child if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the child** (this is the most common situation)
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned you're in Manchester "with the kids," I assume you live with both Alex and Blake - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, I live with Alex here in Manchester. Blake doesn't live with me though - they live with their mum. But I do help out financially with Blake - I contribute towards their upkeep with things like clothes and bits and pieces.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - it's helpful to know the different living arrangements for each child.

So to summarise:
- **Alex (13)**: Lives with you ✓
- **Blake (15)**: Lives with their mum, but you contribute to their upkeep

For Blake, I just need to check one thing: Do your contributions towards Blake's care (clothes, etc.) amount to **at least the same value as the Child Benefit payment** (currently around £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, or £16.95 for additional children)?

This can be weekly, monthly, or even lump sum contributions - and it includes things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, or presents.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute about £26 a week towards Blake's upkeep, so that should cover it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, £26 per week definitely meets the contribution threshold. So you're responsible for both children - Alex by living with them, and Blake through your financial contributions. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. I need to check if anyone else is currently claiming or intending to claim Child Benefit for Alex or Blake.

1. **For Alex** (who lives with you): Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

2. **For Blake** (who lives with their mum): Is Blake's mum currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or does she intend to?

This is particularly important for Blake's situation since they live with their mum - typically the person the child lives with has priority for the claim.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No one else is claiming for Alex - he lives with me so that's straightforward.

As for Blake, their mum isn't claiming Child Benefit for them. Nobody else is getting it for Blake at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, we can proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to check the children's current living situations:**

Are Alex and Blake in any of these special circumstances?

- **In hospital** for an extended period
- **In local authority care**
- **Being fostered**

Or are they both in **normal living arrangements** (i.e., Alex living with you at home, and Blake living at home with their mum)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Alex is living normally at home with me here in Manchester.

Blake is actually in local authority care at the moment - they've been there for a little while now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know about Blake's situation. Since the children have different circumstances, I'll need to check a few more details about Blake being in local authority care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question for Blake's eligibility.

**About Blake being in local authority care:**

Child Benefit can continue while a child is in care, but there are time limits. Does **either** of the following apply to Blake?

1. **Blake has been in care for 8 weeks or less**, OR

2. **Blake spends at least 24 hours per week at home** (with you or their mum)

There's also an exception if Blake spends at least **2 consecutive nights per week** at home, or **7 or more days in a row** at home.

Can you tell me:
- How long has Blake been in local authority care?
- Does Blake spend any regular time at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake has been in care for about a month now - so that's within the 8 weeks. They don't really come home regularly at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Blake has only been in care for about a month (approximately 4 weeks), that's within the 8-week limit, so you can still be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about council payments:**

Is the local council **NOT paying** towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care?

Generally, if the council is paying for a child's accommodation or upkeep, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.

Do you know if the council is covering Blake's costs while they're in care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local council is paying for Blake while they're in care - they're covering the costs of the accommodation and everything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Unfortunately, this affects Blake's eligibility.

Let me now check the final step for Alex, who is living normally with you at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex**, who is living normally with you at home - is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

(I'd expect the answer is no since Alex lives with you at home, but I just need to confirm this final point.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. He just lives here with me normally - I take care of everything for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected. Since the council is not paying for Alex, and he lives with you normally, this completes the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you a final assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (age 13) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in his own name
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- He lives with you
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council is not paying for his care

### ❌ Blake (age 15) - **NOT ELIGIBLE (currently)**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake at this time. While Blake meets most of the requirements, the key issue is:

> **Blake is in local authority care and the council is paying for their accommodation and maintenance.** When the council pays for a child's care, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.

---

## What Happens Next?

**For Alex - How to Claim:**
1. Apply online at [gov.uk](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. You'll need to provide proof of identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. Payments are usually made every 4 weeks

**For Blake - Future Eligibility:**
If Blake's situation changes, you may become eligible to claim for them:
- If Blake leaves care and returns home
- If the council stops paying for Blake's accommodation/maintenance
- If Blake starts spending at least 24 hours per week at home

**Important Note:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid through tax.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your eligibility or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (13) meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) He is under 16 years old - automatic age qualification. 2) He is not working. 3) He is not receiving benefits (ESA/Universal Credit) in his own name. 4) He is not in an apprenticeship. 5) You meet residency requirements (living in the UK in Manchester). 6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. 8) He is in a normal living arrangement at home with you. 9) The local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (15) does NOT meet all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) Blake is under 16 years old - meets age requirement. 2) Blake is not working - meets requirement. 3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own name - meets requirement. 4) Blake is not in an apprenticeship - meets requirement. 5) You meet residency requirements - meets requirement. 6) You contribute £26/week to Blake's upkeep (above the threshold) - meets responsibility requirement. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake - meets requirement. 8) HOWEVER, Blake is in local authority care AND the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance. This makes Blake ineligible - you cannot receive Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying for their care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you and meets all requirements. However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because they are in local authority care with the council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes (e.g., they leave care and return home, or the council stops paying for their accommodation), you may become eligible to claim for Blake at that point.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (13) meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) He is under 16 years old - automatic age qualification. 2) He is not working. 3) He is not receiving benefits (ESA/Universal Credit) in his own name. 4) He is not in an apprenticeship. 5) You meet residency requirements (living in the UK in Manchester). 6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. 8) He is in a normal living arrangement at home with you. 9) The local council is not paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (15) does NOT meet all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) Blake is under 16 years old - meets age requirement. 2) Blake is not working - meets requirement. 3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own name - meets requirement. 4) Blake is not in an apprenticeship - meets requirement. 5) You meet residency requirements - meets requirement. 6) You contribute £26/week to Blake's upkeep (above the threshold) - meets responsibility requirement. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake - meets requirement. 8) HOWEVER, Blake is in local authority care AND the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance. This makes Blake ineligible - you cannot receive Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying for their care.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you and meets all requirements. However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because they are in local authority care with the council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes (e.g., they leave care and return home, or the council stops paying for their accommodation), you may become eligible to claim for Blake at that point.
